Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to study, but then I'd end up doing other things or just not feeling it. When I did study, it was usually hands-on stuff like trying to work through problems myself rather than just reading. I put in about 22 hours a week supposedly, but a lot of that time was probably me just sitting there not really focused.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, man. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and try to cram everything in. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and whether I felt like I could actually handle the work that day. Regular times? That wasn't really my thing.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due or I got some notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, scroll around a bit, and then leave without doing much. It wasn't like I was excited to be there, you know? It was more like checking a box or avoiding getting too far behind.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were maybe a few weeks where I was logging in more regularly, but it never stuck. Something would come up or I'd just lose interest, and then I'd be back to only showing up when absolutely necessary. The LMS just never became something I naturally went to.
Q5: The videos were actually decent because I could watch them and follow along with examples. I learn better when I can see things being done rather than just reading about them. The discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better for me. Those felt more useful than just staring at PDFs.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and extra resources they posted. They just felt like too much, and I couldn't sit still long enough to get through them. If something wasn't directly related to an assignment, I probably skipped it. It's not that I didn't care, I just couldn't make myself engage with all of it.
Q7: If I could see how it was going to help me with an assignment or the exam, I'd actually do it. Activities that let me practice and do something with my hands felt worth it. But if it was just reading for the sake of reading or watching something that didn't seem connected to anything, I'd zone out or skip it entirely.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it because I already felt overwhelmed with the required stuff. Required activities had deadlines and consequences, so I'd at least attempt them. Optional things just fell off my radar completely.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or notes and then study from my phone or laptop without being connected to the LMS. Sometimes I'd work through problems on paper or try to practice things physically. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just messing around with concepts on my own.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours trying to understand something using my notes or even YouTube videos, but none of that would show up in the system. I'd be working through practice problems at my desk, and the LMS would just show me as inactive. My engagement wasn't always digital, you know?
Q11: Yeah, I did that a lot actually. I'd open a PDF or video once, download it or take notes, and then never go back to the LMS for it. I preferred having stuff saved so I could look at it whenever without needing internet or logging in. The LMS probably thought I only looked at it once, but I used those materials multiple times.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to engage. Sometimes it was stress from other stuff in my life, sometimes I'd look at the workload and feel completely overwhelmed. When I felt like I was already too far behind, it was hard to even start. The anxiety would just make me avoid everything.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. If I had other responsibilities or was just mentally exhausted, I'd put off logging in. The confusion about some of the material made it worse because I didn't even know where to start, so I'd just... not.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be on the LMS but not really there mentally. I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I was technically accessing stuff, but I wasn't absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: All the time. I'd know something was important but still put it off because it felt too hard or I didn't know how to approach it. The bigger the task seemed, the more I'd avoid it. It's like my brain would just shut down when faced with something overwhelming, even though I knew I was making things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore stuff until it was almost due, then panic and try to get it done. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done half the things I did. They were stressful but necessary.
Q17: Honestly, I didn't pay much attention to feedback. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally or was stressed about the next thing. When I did look at feedback, it sometimes made me feel worse about myself, so I started avoiding it. It didn't really change how I engaged with the course.
Q18: The structure was kind of confusing sometimes, which made it harder to stay on track. If things were laid out more clearly or broken into smaller pieces, I might have engaged more. When everything felt like one big overwhelming thing, I'd just disengage. The CBT session I had helped me see some of these patterns, but changing them is hard.
Q19: Having videos helped because I could watch and follow along. But the amount of reading and the way some modules were organized made it harder. If there had been more interactive stuff or hands-on activities built in, I probably would have stayed engaged better. I need to do things, not just read about them.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying to figure things out or searching for help, and that shows up as activity. But then when it comes to actually submitting, you might not feel confident enough or you run out of time. Being active doesn't mean you understand the material well enough to produce something.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me. Some people might study better offline or already know the material from somewhere else. I got pretty good scores on my assignments when I actually did them, even though I wasn't always on the LMS. The system doesn't see everything you're doing to learn.
Q22: The log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you were actually focused or learning anything. It misses all the offline studying, the mental struggles, and the times you're engaged but not in a way the system can track. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things. For me, it's when I'm actively trying to figure something out, even if I'm struggling. It's more about your mindset than your activity logs.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how stressed and anxious I was, or how much I was dealing with outside of school. It wouldn't show the times I studied from downloaded materials or practiced things on my own. It also wouldn't show that I actually cared about doing well, even though my attendance and grades don't reflect that. There's a lot going on beneath the surface.
